title: Seven Hills
bullets: Rome began as a cluster of farming villages on seven low hills above a marshy flood plain along the Tiber River, 16 miles inland from the coast of central Italy.
The site controlled the intersection of the north-south corridor between Etruscan cities and Greek colonies and the east-west Via Salaria salt route across the Tiber.
The founding legend dates Rome to April 21, 753 BCE, when Romulus killed his twin brother Remus and named the city after himself on the Palatine Hill.
Virgil's Aeneid gave Rome a second origin story connecting the city to Aeneas, a Trojan prince who fled the fall of Troy and founded the dynasty that produced Romulus.
The Etruscans, the dominant civilization of central Italy, transformed Rome from a village into a city under the last three kings, draining the Forum and building the Temple of Jupiter.
The Cloaca Maxima, one of the world's earliest sewage systems built by Etruscan engineers, drained the marshy valley that became the Roman Forum and is still partly functioning 2,500 years later.
King Servius Tullius built the Servian Wall enclosing all seven hills and reorganized Roman society into wealth-based classes, creating the centuriate assembly.
In 509 BCE the Romans overthrew the tyrant Tarquinius Superbus and swore never to have a king again, replacing monarchy with two annually elected consuls who could veto each other.

title: The Early Republic
bullets: The Conflict of the Orders, a two-century struggle between patricians and plebeians from 494 to 287 BCE, produced the tribune of the plebs, a magistrate with sacred veto power found in no other ancient civilization.
The Twelve Tables of 450 BCE, Rome's first written law code, established the revolutionary principle that law should be public and apply equally to patrician and plebeian alike.
The Lex Hortensia of 287 BCE made plebeian assembly decisions binding on all citizens including patricians, completing two centuries of political struggle for legal equality.
In 390 BCE a Gallic army under Brennus sacked Rome itself, a trauma so deep that the cultural memory of it still justified Caesar's conquest of Gaul 350 years later.
The three Samnite Wars from 343 to 290 BCE established Roman dominance over the Italian peninsula through grinding resilience rather than superior tactics.
Rome's alliance system turned defeated enemies into military assets, binding conquered peoples through a layered system of citizenship, Latin rights, and treaty alliances.

In 312 BCE Appius Claudius Caecus began the Appian Way, Rome's first major road, and the Aqua Appia, Rome's first aqueduct, establishing the infrastructure pattern the empire would replicate across three continents.
By 264 BCE Rome controlled peninsular Italy and could field armies that dwarfed anything the Greek city-states or Hellenistic kingdoms could muster, because half of Italy marched under Roman command.

title: The Punic Wars
bullets: The Punic Wars decided whether the western Mediterranean would be Semitic and Phoenician or Latin and Roman, and the consequences shaped everything from Latin languages to Christianity to the political map of Europe.
Rome built a navy from scratch during the First Punic War, training rowers on dry-land benches and inventing the corvus boarding device to turn naval battles into land fights at sea.
Hannibal Barca crossed the Alps with 50,000 troops and 37 war elephants in 218 BCE, losing nearly half his force in the mountains in one of the most audacious military operations in history.

At Cannae in 216 BCE Hannibal surrounded and annihilated roughly 80,000 Roman soldiers using a double-envelopment maneuver, the highest single-day death toll in Western military history until the Battle of the Somme.
Rome refused to surrender after Cannae, adopted the Fabian strategy of attrition against Hannibal, and contained him in southern Italy for thirteen years without ever defeating him in open battle.
Scipio Africanus defeated Hannibal at the Battle of Zama in 202 BCE by using Hannibal's own tactics, ending the Second Punic War and establishing Rome as the dominant Mediterranean power.
In 146 BCE Rome destroyed both Carthage and Corinth, erasing the Phoenician city so thoroughly that the site was allegedly sown with salt, and absorbing Greece as the province of Achaea.
The Roman poet Horace captured the cultural conquest in reverse: Graecia capta ferum victorem cepit, conquered Greece took captive her savage conqueror.

title: The Late Republic
bullets: Tiberius Gracchus was beaten to death with chair legs in 133 BCE for proposing land reform, the first political murder in the Forum and the beginning of a century of political violence.
Gaius Marius opened military enlistment to the landless poor in 107 BCE, creating a professional army whose soldiers were loyal to their generals rather than the state.
The Social War of 91-87 BCE forced Rome to grant citizenship to all Italian allies, roughly doubling the citizen body overnight after decades of resistance.
Sulla marched his army on Rome twice, conducted proscriptions that killed thousands, and proved that one man with a loyal army could seize and hold power over the Republic.
Spartacus escaped from a gladiatorial school at Capua with seventy companions and built a slave army of 70,000 that defeated multiple Roman legions before being crushed by Crassus.
Crassus crucified 6,000 captured rebels along the Appian Way from Capua to Rome, a 130-mile corridor of crosses that stood as a warning for years.
Cicero, Rome's greatest orator, tried to defend the Republic through words alone, leaving over 900 surviving letters that are among the most important texts of Western civilization.
By 60 BCE three men dominated Roman politics: Pompey the general, Crassus the plutocrat, and Julius Caesar the ambitious patrician with charm, debts, and a plan.

title: Caesar and the Death of the Republic
bullets: The First Triumvirate was a private deal among Caesar, Pompey, and Crassus to pool their resources and ensure nothing happened in Roman politics without their collective approval.
Caesar conquered all of Gaul in eight years, extending Roman control from the Pyrenees to the Rhine and writing the Commentarii de Bello Gallico as real-time propaganda.
Crassus was killed at the Battle of Carrhae in 53 BCE fighting the Parthians, reportedly having molten gold poured down his throat as punishment for his legendary greed.
Caesar crossed the Rubicon on January 10, 49 BCE with one legion, starting a civil war that gave every Western language the phrase crossing the Rubicon for passing the point of no return.
On the Ides of March, 44 BCE, about sixty senators stabbed Caesar to death at the foot of a statue of Pompey, triggering thirteen more years of civil war instead of restoring the Republic.
The Second Triumvirate conducted proscriptions more savage than Sulla's, killing Cicero and displaying his head and hands on the Rostra from which he had delivered his greatest speeches.
Octavian defeated Antony and Cleopatra at the Battle of Actium in 31 BCE, ending the Ptolemaic dynasty that had ruled Egypt since Alexander, and becoming sole ruler of the Roman world at 33.

title: Augustus and the Principate
bullets: Augustus created the Principate by announcing he was restoring the Republic while actually holding supreme power, maintaining the fiction so convincingly that nobody alive could remember what the Republic had looked like.
The Pax Romana from 27 BCE to 180 CE gave the Mediterranean world two centuries of relative peace, connecting 60-70 million people by a road network of over 250,000 miles and two common languages.

Roman concrete, a volcanic ash mixture that could set underwater and actually strengthens over time through chemical reaction with seawater, enabled every dome, arch, vault, and harbor that makes Roman architecture Roman.
Augustus presided over a golden age of Latin literature: Virgil's Aeneid, Ovid's Metamorphoses, Horace's odes, and Livy's history of Rome from its founding.
Egyptian grain fed Rome through the annona, a massive state-run supply chain shipping grain from the Nile Valley to the port of Ostia for distribution to over a million residents.
Roman coins have been found in India, Vietnam, and China, evidence of a Silk Road trade network that reached farther than the legions ever marched.
Nero, the last Julio-Claudian, blamed Christians for the Great Fire of Rome in 64 CE, launching the first organized persecution, then built the lavish Domus Aurea on the cleared land.

The Julio-Claudian dynasty demonstrated the Principate's pathologies: Tiberius withdrew to Capri, Caligula may have planned to make his horse a consul, and Nero gasped What an artist dies in me as he committed suicide.

title: The Flavians and the Five Good Emperors
bullets: The Year of the Four Emperors in 69 CE proved Tacitus's observation that the secret of empire was out: an emperor could be made elsewhere than in Rome.

The Colosseum, built on the site of Nero's private lake, seated 50,000 spectators and featured a retractable canvas awning, underground tunnels, and mechanical lifts raising gladiators through trapdoors.

Mount Vesuvius buried Pompeii and Herculaneum in 79 CE, preserving graffiti, campaign slogans, bread in ovens, and food on tables in the most intimate snapshot of ordinary Roman daily life ever found.

Titus destroyed Jerusalem and the Second Temple in 70 CE, ending the sacrificial cult and accelerating Judaism's transformation into a text-based religion centered on synagogue worship and rabbinic interpretation.

Trajan expanded the empire to its greatest extent, conquering Dacia and briefly Mesopotamia, and was so admired that later senators greeted new emperors with Be luckier than Augustus, better than Trajan.

Hadrian rebuilt the Pantheon with an unreinforced concrete dome 142 feet in diameter, the largest such dome in the world for nearly two millennia and still structurally sound after nineteen centuries.
Hadrian's Wall, a 73-mile fortification across northern Britain, marked the empire's northernmost frontier and served as a customs point, patrol road, and visible statement of Roman authority.

Marcus Aurelius, the philosopher-emperor, wrote the Meditations during military campaigns on the frozen Danube, a personal journal of Stoic philosophy never intended for publication that remains one of the most read ancient works.
The Antonine Plague of 165-180 CE killed an estimated 5 to 10 million people across the empire, weakening it at the precise moment when external pressures were increasing.

title: Crisis and Recovery
bullets: Commodus renamed the months after himself, fought as a gladiator in the Colosseum, and was strangled in his bath on New Year's Eve 192 CE, ending the era of the Five Good Emperors.
Septimius Severus, the first emperor from Africa, fought his way to power and told his sons on his deathbed: Enrich the soldiers, ignore everyone else.
The Edict of Caracalla in 212 CE extended Roman citizenship to all free inhabitants of the empire, probably motivated by the need to expand the tax base rather than idealism.
The Crisis of the Third Century saw over twenty emperors in fifty years, the capture of Emperor Valerian by the Sassanid king Shapur I, and the fragmentation of the empire into three rival states.
Zenobia of Palmyra carved out an independent empire controlling Egypt, Syria, and much of Anatolia, while a breakaway Gallic Empire controlled the western provinces.
The silver content of the denarius fell from 95% pure under Augustus to less than 5% by the 260s, destroying savings and disrupting trade networks that had operated smoothly for two centuries.
Diocletian created the Tetrarchy, dividing the empire among four co-rulers, roughly doubled the number of provinces, and separated civil from military authority in each one.
Diocletian transformed the emperor from Augustus's humble princeps into dominus et deus, borrowing Sassanid Persian court ceremony to make it harder for any random general to claim the throne.

title: Constantine and Christianity
bullets: Christianity had grown from a Jewish sect following the crucifixion of Jesus around 30 CE to perhaps 10-15 percent of the empire's population by the time of Diocletian's persecution.
Paul of Tarsus traveled the eastern Mediterranean between 45 and 64 CE founding churches and articulating the theological framework that distinguished Christianity from its Jewish parent religion.
Constantine won the Battle of the Milvian Bridge in 312 CE after reportedly seeing a cross of light in the sky with the words In hoc signo vinces, In this sign conquer.
The Edict of Milan in 313 CE ended the persecution of Christians and established the revolutionary principle that the state would not persecute people for their religious beliefs.
The Council of Nicaea in 325 CE produced the Nicene Creed, defining Christ as of one substance with the Father and establishing the pattern of emperor as protector and arbiter of Christianity.
Constantinople, founded in 330 CE on the site of ancient Byzantium, was designed as a Christian city from the start and would not fall to a foreign enemy for over eleven centuries.
Emperor Julian the Apostate attempted to restore traditional paganism in 361-363 CE but was killed fighting the Sassanid Persians after only twenty months, and no subsequent emperor tried again.
Theodosius I issued the Edict of Thessalonica in 380 CE making Nicene Christianity the sole legal religion, banned pagan sacrifice, and submitted to excommunication by Bishop Ambrose of Milan.
Augustine of Hippo wrote the Confessions, the first Western autobiography, and the City of God, which argued that Rome's fall proved the inadequacy of earthly power.

title: The Fall
bullets: Odoacer deposed the last Western emperor Romulus Augustulus on September 4, 476 CE and sent the imperial regalia to Constantinople, ending the Western Roman Empire not with a thunderclap but with a shrug.
The Visigoths under Alaric sacked Rome in 410 CE, the first time a foreign enemy had taken the city in 800 years, prompting Jerome to write: The city which had taken the whole world was itself taken.
The Battle of Adrianople in 378 CE destroyed a Roman army and killed Emperor Valens, and the empire never recovered the lost legions, increasingly relying on Germanic foederati to defend its borders.
Attila the Hun terrorized both halves of the empire, was stopped at the Battle of the Catalaunian Plains in 451 CE, and died in 453 reportedly of a nosebleed on his wedding night.
The Vandals sacked Rome for two weeks in 455 CE, stripping temples and palaces of everything portable, including treasures Titus had taken from the Temple of Jerusalem nearly four centuries earlier.
The Codex Theodosianus of 438 CE systematized centuries of Roman imperial law into a single collection that would shape every legal system in continental Europe.

The Western Empire fell because too many things went wrong simultaneously: military pressure, economic decline, political dysfunction, currency debasement, lost provinces, and a frontier too long to defend.
Rome did not fall so much as transform: the empire became the church, Latin became the Romance languages, the law became Europe's common inheritance, and the Pontifex Maximus became the Pope.
